title: Lesson 405
transcript: In relation to vulnerability management and penetration testing, you may have come across these terms, red team, blue team, and purple team. What is this? So red team, these are your simulated attackers. So in a good sized organization, you might have a group of a team of people that are focused on essentially being the simulated attackers in the organization. So the red team is there to be the threat. The red team is doing offensive security. They're doing things like ethical hacking and penetration testing, and they're doing things like simulated spear phishing attacks, you know, internal phishing campaigns. maybe baiting attacks and they're often sort of keeping an eye on what are the latest greatest threats that are out there and thinking about how they can be applied to the organization so essentially the red team is focused on being the threat The blue team, this is your defenders. The blue team is the folks that are focused on stopping the threat. So the blue team are running the security operations. They're monitoring systems and monitoring log files, SIM systems, alerts that are being generated, handling incidents when they happen. right? The blue team is your defenders and they're trying to stop the threat. So that's, if you've ever heard these terms, red team, blue team, that's the difference, right? Red team is your simulated attackers And blue team are the defenders. And by the way, a bit of trivia behind this, right? Where do these terms come from? They're actually from the military. I think it was the US military. They play war games, right? One of the best ways of sort of practicing is to play war games. And so, of course, the blue team, that's the good guys, right? The Americans. And then the red team is, you know, the evil Soviets. So that's where the terms come from. Blue team is the Americans. They're the good guys. And then the red team is the evil Soviet threat. So that's where these terms come from. Now, you may have also heard of purple team, and this is actually a really kind of misnamed thing, because purple team is not actually usually a separate team. Instead, what purple is, is getting your red team and blue teams to work together, to collaborate. Like, yes, it's good to have different teams focused on different things and even to have a little bit of friendly competition. But you don't want it to get to the point where, you know, these teams just do not talk to each other and they don't play nicely. You want them to collaborate. You want the red team to be saying, you know, hey, through our threat intelligence and through what we were finding here, we're finding these issues. and telling the blue team folks so that they can go, okay, good. Now that we know these issues exist, we can go and mitigate those vulnerabilities. We can do something about it. So that's what purple is all about. Getting your blue teams, you know, like, yes, you want some friendly competition. but you also want your red team and your blue team to work together collaboratively, to learn from each other, to share information. And that's what PRPL is all about.
